item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Highly underrated. It’s such a brutal and creepy concept, and while there’s a few similar SCPS (SCP-575 for example), I personally prefer this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main idea is wonderfully simple and creepy, so I'm confused by the whole "last words" addition

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, it's weird and creepy, but like others have said, that redaction seems a little strange, and "I'm sorry" seems cliché. You have a great concept, it just needs a little tuning up.

item.title: They say, when there’s no more room in churches, the choir directors will walk the earth...
(no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept, especially that no visual/physical thing has been recorded. But the awkward run-on in the first description of the dismembering and focus on shock value made me downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
resulting in immediate or delayed death in 100% of observed cases

I'm pretty sure every SCP in existence is this. Also, everything you come into contact with. - Unless you're immortal. It's a really awkward phrasing is what I'm saying.

"The resulting trauma has proven fatal in every case, either from the attack itself or by victims succumbing to blood loss and tissue damage before help can be administered" or something else kinda sciencey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted primarily on the strength of the majority of the article and the overall creepiness factor (honestly, it wouldn't even have to get violent for me to find a something whistling in the dark where there isn't supposed to be a something), but I really feel the whole "I'm sorry" part is uneccessary and detracts from the main thrust, which would stand perfectly well on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote for me. The concept is interesting but the execution is just kind of… heh. Here are my gripes wih it:

the containment procedures seem weird to me. if the SCP is only active in complete darkness, why would the foundation go out of its way to keep it in an active state by making its containment area pitch dark? The way the article is written, leaving it in light wouldn't even destroy it, just make it unable to harm people, a.k.a. actually containing it instead of giving it a chance to escape.

I also think margins of errors should be considered. What I mean by that is that if the SCP can only exist in an area of at least 2m3, the minimum containment area should be slightly bigger; similarly, if the SCP can only transfer within a 1 km range, why take an unnecessary risk by making the minimum distance from an "occupied dark area" 1 km? Make it 2 and you're safe.

In fact, a much safer and much more efficient containment procedure would be to just put it in a single constantly illuminated circular room/warehouse of 2 km in diameter and have people check the lights and replace the ones that occasionally break (which wouldn't make it dark enough for the SCP to become active) and boom, SCP safely contained indefinitely, and you can still induce darkness for testing purposes.

I am also more then a little confused as to how exactly SCP-1219 was "transferred" to its containment site. At first I assumed the foundation managed to exploit its teleporting ability to trap it in a room, but the way it's written, it seems they tossed it in a truck and then delivered it to its current location… somehow, despite having no physical or at least tangible form.

And as many people have said, the second acquisition log is completely unnecessary as well. It adds nothing. If anything, the whole "I'm so sorry" thing feels cliché. I also don't see what justifies the redaction.

So yeah, not downvoting because I feel it's a good idea, but it needs major rewrites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A similar thing happens in "The Wire"

omar comin yo
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang flabbit, it's in my head now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is only one thing that I like. And that is whistling in the dark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No termination attempts are scheduled at this time.

This is a given, as the Foundation usually doesn't destroy skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think 'Ghost Hunters' conforms to Foundation formality standards, dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I heard a folk story like this, but it was with Ol' Susanna instead of Camptown Races.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely like the concept and thought it was enough for an upvote. I like the use of ghost hunters to find it, as well, but like many, I'm stumped/disappointed by the spoiler twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should be 2m3, not 23m, there are about half a dozen of those throughout.

I like the idea of not knowing if it's a entity or event, it has a good science-y, "assume nothing" feel; but this is pretty clearly an entity. It was succesfully moved, ergo it has some sort of form outside of when the anomalous events are actively occuring, therefore it is not just an event.

I got a kick out of the Ghost Hunters cameo. Realistically, the Foundation should be getting at least a few hits off such shows.

Why reprint a whole log back-to-back to add a single line? If you are set on keeping the final words separate, which I don't think is necessary, just tell us the line. No need to reiterate the whole sequence we just got done reading to add on three broken words.

Net result: Good enough and entertained me, so +1. But room for improvements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The contrast between the effects (Hope I spelled that right.) and the object itself are delightful. It's this hazardous, serial killing sound. And it goes all the doo dah day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the tone and the synchrony with Foundation behaviour…

No termination attempts are scheduled at this time.

Why to say that? It's about "Secure, Contain, PROTECT", even considering termination is only when the object is some uncontainable Keter thing.

Is this- here we go. I'm all set up in the second bedroom in apartment

Sounds too informal even for a log.

And things like these all over the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so, what I don't understand is, you in the beginning describe it as "an entity or event". It literally cannot be both. If it's an entity, then your containment procedures I kind of understand. But you cannot contain an event. What do you box up and move, the darkness in the room? And if it's an entity, then you need to describe it, in some way. An entity cannot be "indescribable", because you're not H.P. Lovecraft.

Downvote. I'm so frustrated by this, too, because I enjoy the premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So wait, do they just keep a mute D-Class in the room at all times?

Also, with the lungs removed, it wouldn't be possible to say "I'm Sorry."

It has potential though. -1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the Foundation doesn't destroy, destroy, destroy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps I'm missing something, but if "[r]esearchers speculate that SCP-1219 may be permanently incapacitated via constant illumination of this 1km area…", why wouldn't they just install some kind of light(s) that would keep it at bay? I know the motto is not "destroy, destroy, destroy" but I don't see the upside of keeping it around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Nother thing what kills you dead, doo-dah, doo-dah…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this article, but it feels like it's lacking something currently. As stated before, the requirement for keeping the lighting levels < .0001 lux is preposterous. Even the containment procedures seem over the top; 1 km is quite a large distance for such a small, relatively safe SCP, especially since it doesn't necessarily create a compulsion. Finally, the final uncensored dialogue really is just unnecessary. Overall, it could use some work. No vote from me right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. The volume measurements are entirely wrong (2 cubic meters is 2 m3, not 23m).
  2. Per wikipedia, a lux level of 0.0001 (10-4) is equivalent to the illumination that something would receive by starlight in an overcast sky. ANY artificial light source would produce more than that. The battery light on that camera would've produced more than that.
  3. You actually are appropriately using sound volumes. Congrats on that, a lot of people don't.

Overall, I find this entire thing very meh. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That may be the strangest comparison I've ever seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone, formatting and narrative flow are very solid, and it's effectively creepy, but it just doesn't grab me. Were this made back in the days when The SCP Series 1 still had a bunch of blank spots, I might've upvoted without hesitation, but I just feel like this needs something more before I can. No vote for now.

If this SCP were a girl I was on a date with, it would be as if she was someone who is kind and pretty, but not someone with whom I have any real chemistry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kept expecting some sort of twist or something interesting, but the cloesest thing was "generic 'spooky last words'". It's basically a creepypasta as an SCP. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because I can't get the song out of my head right now and I'm a little scared to start humming it to myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh, this failed to grab me in any way and the Ghost Hunter's death log came across as kind of lame. -1.

On the good side, tone was solid, and the audio log definitely read like something from one of those shows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful; who knew - Ghost Hunters actually sometimes helps humanity even though they dont' know or care!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's restricted so as not to potentially pollute the pool of test subjects with the information in advance.

But the test subjects aren't going to be reading the database entry, and the researchers know to not give information to the test subjects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'determined to be minimum 23m'

Is that supposed to be 2 cubic metres (in which case it should be written as 2m3) or 8m, just written in a silly way?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See my answer to Khym above, which answers a few questions, and the article revisions that resulted. I appreciate the input whether or not you feel able to +1 with the revisions - if you have any other critique I'd be glad to take it under advisement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first should be 'occupied dark area.' 1219 may exist in those caves while in its passive state (or in, for example, unoccupied offices), but seems only to manifest in its active form in occupied spaces. I'll correct that as well as the other distance-related error.

I considered including a test log that would answer those three questions and others - in fact, it's written up - but it seemed unwieldy. If you truly believe the article would benefit from the supplementary material, then I may post it.

It's restricted so as not to potentially pollute the pool of test subjects with the information in advance. Results will be more scientifically pure that way.

Thank you for your input; I was glad to see it because I've seen some of your other critiques and you're generally spot-on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I need to love it in order to +1. For the exact same reasons Khym mentioned, I'll refrain from voting for now. My biggest gripe is the near=impossible containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Freaking nice! Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this disquieting. I like too the part about the 'paranormal' investigators getting their comeuppance .
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
situated at least 1 kilometer from any other dark area.

I don't think the foundation could manage that while the thing is on Earth, unless you change it "enclosed dark area". Even then, how could they make sure there weren't any 23m caves 1 km underground? Changing it to "enclosed artificial dark area" would make it managable, but that would still require its own dedicated site.

However, the song will cease within 10-20 seconds if a witness or witnesses attempt to whistle, hum, sing, or otherwise engage with SCP-1219's tune, and the witness(es) will be dismembered (stripped primarily of organs related to vocalization)

  1. What if they just talk over the whistle, totally unrealted to the tune? Or say something like "Who's there?"
  2. What if they sing a different song?
  3. What if they turn on a recording of the same song?

SCP-1219 has been found capable of instantaneous transfer within an area of approximately 12km.

That should be "within a distance of approximately 1km."

Acquisition Document 1219-1b-2 access restricted to personnel with Level 3 Clearance and above

Why is the fact that the subjects appologize something that's restricted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PNKabHwFs_c

Weird how you can find really specific stuff sometimes.

Anyways, I like this, +1.
